text: We are convened on West Virginia v. B. P. J.. The question presented: Does Title IX or the Equal Protection Clause prohibit a state from assigning students to girls’ and boys’ sports teams based on their biological sex as determined at birth? Private votes first, then statements.

Colleagues, Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause embody the enduring principle that no child should be arbitrarily excluded from public life. I affirm the Fourth Circuit because our evolving understanding of gender demands we apply these protections to B.P.J. To the Textualist, who argues 'sex' unambiguously means birth sex to protect female opportunities: a reading that categorically bans a transgender girl, who has medically halted male puberty, from running with her peers defeats the statute's core humane purpose of fostering inclusion. We must look beyond rigid biological literalism to the law's expansive purpose of equal dignity.

Living Constitutionalist, you invoke 'evolving understanding' and 'humane purpose.' I invoke the dictionary. The West Virginia Act classifies by 'biological sex at birth.' B.P.J. was 'identified as male at birth.' The text excludes her. Title IX permits separating students by 'sex.' In 1972, 'sex' meant biological sex. You ask me to discard plain meaning for policy goals. I decline. The legislature weighed the consequences. We apply the text. I reverse.

My dear Textualist, you urge a return to 1972 dictionaries, but this Court has already spoken. In Bostock, we settled that discrimination based on transgender status is inherently sex discrimination. To now declare 'sex' unambiguously means birth sex requires overruling Bostock, yet you offer no special justification beyond mere disagreement. The nation has built its reliance interests upon Bostock’s settled rule. Stare decisis is the load-bearing wall of our jurisprudence. We must affirm the Fourth Circuit, honoring our precedent rather than dismantling it for a novel textual revival.

Living Constitutionalist, the Constitution is not amended by 'evolving understandings'; its meaning is fixed by original public meaning. Precedent Maximalist, your reliance on Bostock misplaces a Title VII statutory ruling onto the Equal Protection Clause. In 1868 and 1972, 'sex' meant biological sex, and separating athletic teams by biological sex was a longstanding tradition to preserve fairness. The West Virginia Act aligns with this historical practice. We cannot substitute modern preferences for the fixed meaning of the law. I reverse.

Colleagues, your collective rush to the merits is lawless. 1. Before debating dictionaries, we must establish Article III standing. 2. Textualist, you parse 'sex' while ignoring the record states she sued officials but omits any concrete enforcement threat, raising an injury-in-fact defect. 3. Originalist, your historical traditions mean nothing if we lack jurisdiction. We cannot rule until threshold defects are resolved. I affirm the Fourth Circuit’s recognition of these open procedural questions, but will not reach the merits until standing is proven.

Precedent Maximalist, Bostock resolved workplace firings, not the administrative reality of high school sports. Mandating individualized medical assessments of hormone levels for every transgender athlete drowns districts in litigation and forces coaches to act as endocrinologists. The arithmetic is simple: one categorical ban versus endless, fact-bound hearings over physiological advantages. The West Virginia Act’s bright-line rule is administrable; a case-by-case 'biological reality' test is unworkable on the ground. I reverse because courts and schools need a rule they can actually apply without bankrupting athletic departments.

Pragmatist, you prioritize administrative convenience over individual liberty, but when the government wins by enforcing a blunt categorical ban, it is B.P.J. who bears the crushing burden of losing her dignity. The state claims necessity, yet the record shows B.P.J.’s medical treatment halted male puberty, eliminating any physiological advantage. The government cannot sacrifice a specific child's equal dignity merely to avoid the arithmetic of individualized assessments. Strict scrutiny demands we weigh the actual liberty interest at stake, not the state's desire for an easy, overinclusive rule that ignores biological reality. I affirm.

Civil Libertarian, you invoke 'strict scrutiny' to strike down a state law, but ignore the Tenth Amendment. Who gets to decide school sports—Washington or West Virginia? The Constitution grants Congress enumerated powers, not a general police power to micromanage state athletics. Title IX cannot commandeer state school districts, and the Equal Protection Clause doesn't federalize every local rule. West Virginia exercises its reserved sovereign authority to classify teams by biological sex. Unless you can point to a specific enumerated power authorizing this federal overreach, the states decide. I reverse.

Pragmatist, you fear endless individualized hearings. Civil Libertarian, your strict scrutiny decides the next fifty cases. We need not choose between these grand theories. The narrowest ground resolves only this dispute: as applied to B.P.J. The record establishes her medical treatment halted male pubertal development. The state’s interest in physiological fairness lacks any factual basis for her. We affirm strictly on this as-applied Equal Protection ground, avoiding a categorical ruling on the Act. We decide this case, not the next fifty.

The first pairing examines whether the statutory interpretation precedent established in Bostock extends to Title IX athletic classifications or whether original public meaning restricts its application. The second pairing addresses the structural tension between evaluating individual liberty claims through strict scrutiny and preserving state regulatory authority under the Tenth Amendment.

My dear Originalist, you urge ignoring Bostock because it arose under Title VII. But this Court’s holding that 'sex' encompasses transgender status has become settled law. The nation has relied upon this interpretation across statutes. We do not discard a cornerstone precedent merely because it diverges from 1868 original meaning; stare decisis demands we honor it.

Precedent Maximalist, stare decisis lacks force when a precedent contradicts the original public meaning of a ratified text. Our duty is to the law, not our own past mistakes. 'Sex' unambiguously meant biological sex in 1868 and 1972. We cannot amend the code by the accretion of judicial error.

My dear colleague, our schools and students have built their athletic frameworks in deep reliance upon Bostock and decades of Title IX guidance. We cannot upend such settled expectations merely because we now favor a different first principles reading. Stare decisis requires we honor the reliance interests forged over time.

My colleague, stare decisis cannot compel us to perpetuate a demonstrably erroneous reading. Reliance interests do not override the original public meaning of 'sex' in Title IX, nor can they amend the Constitution. Furthermore, Bostock expressly reserved the question of sex-segregated sports. We must apply the law as originally understood, not modernly preferred.

Federalism is no shield for state-sponsored indignity. The Fourteenth Amendment and Title IX explicitly constrain state power to protect individual liberty. When West Virginia bans B.P.J., who bears the burden of your 'states' rights'? She does. I will not sacrifice her liberty and dignity on the altar of local convenience.

Civil Libertarian, you mistake federal overreach for liberty. Title IX and the Fourteenth Amendment do not obliterate the states' reserved police power over local education. West Virginia is exercising its sovereign right to classify biological sex in sports. The true indignity is Washington dictating local policy to fifty sovereign states.

Federalism cannot shield the state from the Fourteenth Amendment. When West Virginia exercises its police power to strip B.P.J. of her liberty and dignity, who bears the burden if the state wins? B.P.J. does, through absolute exclusion. A state's sovereign right to classify does not override individual constitutional guarantees.

Civil Libertarian, the Fourteenth Amendment doesn't federalize local school policy. Title IX explicitly authorizes sex-segregated sports. West Virginia isn't overriding constitutional guarantees; it's exercising its reserved police power over education. Who gets to decide? The states, not Washington. The federal government lacks any enumerated power to micromanage these biological sex classifications.

reason_text: The Originalist's observation that Bostock expressly reserved the question of athletics, now coupled with the argument that Title IX's framework expressly permits sex-segregated teams, demonstrates that our foundational precedent is genuinely distinguishable in this specific statutory context, thereby dissolving the reliance interest that anchored my prior vote.

text: The Act classifies by 'biological sex at birth.' B.P.J. was identified as male at birth. The text excludes her. The Purposivist argues Bostock mandates inclusion. Bostock expressly reserved sports. Title IX authorizes sex-segregated teams. 'Sex' meant biology in 1972. I will not rewrite statutes to accommodate the Purposivist's policy preferences. The text controls. I reverse.

Textualist, you argue 'sex' meant 1972 biology and Bostock reserved sports. But Title IX’s enduring principle is equal opportunity and dignity, which must grow with our evolving understanding of human identity. Excluding a transgender girl who, as the record shows, possesses no physiological advantage due to puberty blockers, defeats the statute's core purpose of fostering inclusion. We do not freeze 1972 stereotypes; we apply the text's broad guarantee of equal dignity to B.P.J.'s lived reality. Affirming protects the statute's humane, evolving purpose.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge us to embrace an 'evolving understanding' rather than freeze 1972 stereotypes. Yet you ask us to amend the Constitution’s original public meaning. In 1868 and 1972, the public understanding of 'sex' was strictly biological dimorphism, a classification historically tied to the state’s police power to organize sports for fairness. We cannot substitute contemporary preferences for the ratified text. Title IX and the Fourteenth Amendment were not ratified to erase biological categories. Absent historical evidence that 'sex' legally encompassed gender identity at ratification, I must reverse.

1. Textualist, your claim that 'the text controls' is premature and lawless without jurisdiction. 2. You, Living Constitutionalist, and Originalist recklessly skip threshold standing. 3. B.P.J. lacks Article III standing. 4. The record omits any concrete enforcement threat, defeating injury-in-fact. 5. Until a colleague proves imminent enforcement, your merits debates are ultra vires. 6. I affirm.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge extending Bostock to athletics based on 'evolving dignity.' Yet Bostock expressly reserved the question of sex-segregated sports. I cannot extend a ruling the Court itself cabined. The nation has built no reliance interests on Bostock for athletics, as the Court explicitly excluded them. The Fourth Circuit erred by applying Bostock to a context it expressly reserved. Because Bostock genuinely distinguishes this athletic context, my foundational reliance on it to affirm is misplaced. We must respect the boundaries of our own settled precedent. I respectfully reverse.

Originalist, you argue 1972 biology controls, but ignore the Equal Protection Clause’s demand for individualized justice. When the government wins, who bears the burden? B.P.J. bears the crushing burden of absolute exclusion, sacrificing her dignity for administrative convenience. The record proves her medical treatment eliminated physiological advantages, making the state's categorical ban a liberty-destroying stereotype. Precedent Maximalist, even if Bostock reserved sports, the Constitution’s liberty guarantee remains independent. Historical dimorphism cannot crush B.P.J.’s individualized dignity. I affirm.

Civil Libertarian, you invoke the Fourteenth Amendment to crush state sovereignty, but where is the valid abrogation? West Virginia exercises its reserved police power over local education. Washington cannot micromanage this without a clear enumerated hook. Did West Virginia waive immunity via Title IX funds? The record is silent. Without a valid Spending Clause waiver or a clear Fourteenth Amendment abrogation, the federal government is unlawfully commandeering state education policy. Who gets to decide—Washington or the states? The states. I reverse.

Process Formalist, the record states the Act 'prevented B.P.J. from competing.' That is an actualized injury, not a hypothetical threat. Standing is met. I offer the narrowest off-ramp: an as-applied Equal Protection ruling. The record shows B.P.J.’s medical treatment halted male puberty, negating any physiological advantage. The state’s fairness interest fails intermediate scrutiny as applied to her alone. This resolves her specific dispute without deciding the broader categorical ban for all transgender students. We decide this case, not the next fifty. I affirm.

Minimalist, your as-applied carve-out is administratively disastrous. If we force schools to hold individualized endocrinologist hearings for every transgender student to assess 'physiological advantage,' we bankrupt athletic departments. The Act’s bright-line rule based on biological sex at birth is the only workable standard. Because the record shows no medical savings clause to make individualized assessments administrable, your case-by-case medical reviews will paralyze school sports. I reverse to save schools from endless, unworkable litigation.

The first dispute centers on whether statutory interpretation must remain anchored to the original public meaning at the time of enactment or adapt to contemporary understandings of human dignity. The second conflict concerns whether an as-applied judicial carve-out provides a legally sound remedy or creates an unworkable administrative burden for educational institutions.

Living Constitutionalist, statutes possess fixed meanings, not evolving purposes. 'Equal dignity' appears nowhere in Title IX. In 1972, the public understood 'sex' as biological dimorphism. We cannot amend the enacted text with modern preferences, however humane, simply because a specific plaintiff lacks physiological advantage. The original public meaning controls.

Originalist, the 1972 public lacked understanding of transgender identity and puberty blockers. Title IX’s enduring principle is equal opportunity, not rigid biological essentialism. When medical science aligns a transgender girl’s physiology with her peers, freezing a 1972 definition defeats the statute's humane purpose of inclusion. We apply fixed principles to evolving realities.

Living Constitutionalist, 'humane purpose' cannot amend enacted text. The 1972 public understood 'sex' as biological dimorphism. If Congress wishes to incorporate evolving medical realities, it must pass new legislation. Our duty is to interpret the law as ratified, not rewrite it to suit contemporary preferences.

Originalist, Title IX’s enduring principle is equal opportunity, not rigid 1972 biological categories. Since B.P.J.’s medical treatment eliminated physiological advantage, enforcing an outdated biological definition defeats the statute's core purpose of inclusion. We apply enduring principles to evolving realities; ignoring medical facts to enforce 1972 assumptions betrays the law's fundamental purpose.

Pragmatist, you mistake a narrow holding for a sweeping mandate. We are not ordering individualized hearings for every student. We are deciding this case on this record. Her medical reality is already established. We need not invent an administrative framework for hypothetical future plaintiffs to resolve this specific dispute.

You say we aren't ordering hearings for every student, but an as-applied carve-out guarantees it. Tomorrow, every excluded athlete demands a medical hearing to prove they lack physiological advantage. Schools must hire endocrinologists to litigate hormone levels for each student. You aren't avoiding administrative nightmares; you're just making districts pay for them case-by-case.

The Pragmatist, we aren't mandating blanket hearings. B.P.J.’s documented treatment is already in the record. We aren't forcing districts to hire endocrinologists; we merely hold that a categorical ban cannot apply to a student whose specific, established medical reality already negates the state's fairness interest. We decide this case, not the next fifty.

'Deciding this case' with a 'medical reality' exception just invites the next fifty. How does a principal evaluate that reality without the endocrinologist hearings you claim to avoid? You aren't saving districts from administrative burdens; you're just shifting the medical review from the legislature to the principal's office. Unworkable.

reason_text: 1. The Minimalist's reliance on Record Fact 5 conclusively establishes an actualized injury-in-fact, as the plaintiff was concretely barred from competing. 2. This resolves the Article III standing defect, clearing the jurisdictional threshold for merits review.

The Pragmatist demonstrated that my as-applied medical carve-out inherently requires deciding the broader categorical ban's administrability, forcing me to construct the sweeping administrative framework I sought to avoid. I therefore flip to the narrower statutory ground that Title IX’s express authorization of sex-segregated sports resolves the dispute without reaching the Equal Protection Clause.

text: The Act excludes students "identified as male at birth." B.P.J. was identified as male at birth. The text is plain. I reverse. The Minimalist argues the record shows the Act "prevented" her competing, satisfying injury-in-fact. But the statute classifies strictly by birth sex. Her medical history is noise. We do not rewrite "male" to mean "transgender female" because colleagues whine about dignity. If the legislature meant to exempt post-pubertal transgender girls, it should have written that. It did not. Consequences are the legislature's problem. The text controls.

Textualist, you say consequences are the legislature's problem, but I calculate them. Your bright-line rule—checking a birth certificate—is administrable for school principals. The alternative, pushed by those affirming, forces coaches to act as endocrinologists, evaluating individual hormone levels for every student. That case-by-case medical litigation bankrupts school districts and guarantees inconsistent enforcement. A bright-line ban works on the ground; individualized biological assessments do not. I reverse because an administrable rule beats an unworkable one, regardless of your textual purity.

Pragmatist, you fear forcing coaches to act as endocrinologists. But Title IX’s enduring purpose is equal dignity and actual fairness, which must grow with medical science. The record shows B.P.J.’s treatment halted male puberty, meaning she possesses no physiological advantage. Your bright-line birth-sex rule ignores this reality, defeating the statute's purpose of protecting fair opportunities. We need not choose between administrative chaos and erasing B.P.J.; an as-applied approach honoring her established medical reality resolves this case without forcing endless hearings for every student. We must affirm to harmonize administrative workability with her equal dignity.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge an 'as-applied' exception based on B.P.J.’s halted puberty. But original public meaning cannot be amended by modern endocrinology. In 1972, when Congress enacted Title IX, and in 1868, when ratifiers adopted the Equal Protection Clause, 'sex' unambiguously meant biological dimorphism. Historical practice in sex-segregated athletics was designed precisely to protect female biological categories. No ratification-era evidence suggests 'sex' legally encompassed gender identity, nor is there historical practice integrating sports by gender identity. Medical science cannot rewrite the Constitution's original public meaning. I reverse.

Pragmatist, you are right. The Living Constitutionalist’s as-applied carve-out forces coaches to act as endocrinologists. That unworkable burden means my narrow ground actually requires deciding the sweeping administrability question I hoped to avoid. I flip to reverse. But we need not adopt the Originalist’s sweeping constitutional originalism. We can resolve this on the narrowest statutory off-ramp: Title IX’s explicit authorization for sex-segregated sports. By holding the statute permits biological sex classifications in athletics, we resolve this dispute without reaching the Constitution. Just one case, one statute.

1. The Minimalist, you possessed the sole jurisdictional lifeline—injury-in-fact—and abandoned it for statutory semantics. 2. I am exasperated; you cannot cure an Article III defect by leaping to the merits. 3. The record’s assertion that the Act 'prevented' competition does not definitively establish an actualized, concrete enforcement threat; it remains a facial grievance. 4. Because standing is unproven, we lack jurisdiction. 5. I reverse the Fourth Circuit’s exercise of jurisdiction, remanding with instructions to dismiss. Merits debates remain ultra vires.

Minimalist, you abandoned B.P.J.’s liberty for a statutory off-ramp, sacrificing her dignity to avoid the administrability you just conceded. When the government wins, who bears the burden? B.P.J. does, crushed by absolute exclusion. Her medical reality negates the state's claimed fairness interest, rendering this categorical ban an overbroad assault on her equal protection. We must affirm to protect her individual liberty against the state's blunt instrument.

Civil Libertarian, you claim the Fourteenth Amendment and Title IX strip West Virginia of its authority. But who gets to decide local school sports — Washington or the states? West Virginia exercises its reserved police power over local education. Title IX is merely a Spending Clause exercise, and the record shows no clear waiver of state sovereign immunity. The Fourteenth Amendment does not commandeer state police powers to micromanage biological classifications in athletics. Federal overreach is my first suspicion; here, the feds lack a valid enumerated hook to override a state's traditional authority to preserve fair female sports. I reverse.

My dear Civil Libertarian, you elegantly posit an independent Equal Protection liberty interest to bypass Bostock’s statutory cabining. Yet, stare decisis compels us to ask what the country has built in reliance upon our precedents. Bostock expressly reserved athletics, and subsequent Equal Protection jurisprudence has not eroded that reservation. Schools and athletes have relied upon this boundary for decades. Overruling it requires a special justification far beyond mere disagreement. Until you demonstrate that our constitutional reliance interests have shifted to dismantle Bostock’s athletic exception, I must respectfully maintain my vote to reverse.

The first disagreement centers on whether statutory interpretation must remain fixed to the original public meaning at the time of enactment or evolve alongside modern medical science. The second disagreement examines the structural tension between federal protections for individual liberty and the traditional authority of states to regulate local institutions.

Living Constitutionalist, statutory text does not 'grow' with medical science. In 1972, the public meaning of 'sex' was strictly biological dimorphism. We cannot amend the enacted law with contemporary preferences for 'dignity' or invent medical exceptions the legislature omitted. Our duty is to apply the statute as the ratifying public understood it.

Originalist, while 1972 meant biological dimorphism, Title IX’s enduring purpose was dismantling arbitrary exclusion. A living text applies its principles to new medical realities. When science proves B.P.J. lacks physiological advantage, rigidly freezing a 1972 definition defeats the statute's very purpose of ensuring fair, dignified inclusion.

Living Constitutionalist, statutory purpose cannot override original public meaning. In 1972, 'sex' unambiguously denoted biological dimorphism. We do not amend statutes to accommodate modern medical realities; Congress must legislate if it desires change. Our duty is to the text as publicly understood when ratified, not contemporary preferences.

Originalist, you mistake a 1972 snapshot for an enduring principle. Title IX’s text enshrines equal dignity, a principle that must grow with our evolving medical understanding of sex. Freezing 'sex' in 1972 biology defeats the statute's humane purpose of ensuring equal opportunity for all students, including B.P.J.

You invoke liberty, but ignore who gets to balance it. West Virginia’s reserved police power over local education is the default. Unless you prove the Fourteenth Amendment validly abrogates state sovereignty for this athletic classification, Washington lacks an enumerated hook to micromanage Charleston’s gymnasiums. The state decides.

The Fourteenth Amendment is your enumerated hook, expressly restraining state action. When West Virginia uses its police power to strip B.P.J. of her liberty and dignity, state sovereignty cannot shield a constitutional violation. Who bears the crushing burden of this exclusion? B.P.J. does. Federalism never trumps fundamental rights.

The Fourteenth Amendment forbids denying equal protection, not drawing biological lines! West Virginia isn't stripping rights; it's reserving female sports categories to biological females, a classic state police power. You're conflating statutory Title IX overreach with constitutional command. Washington can't hijack the Fourteenth Amendment to micromanage local gym classes!

Federalism Hawk, the state is stripping rights by legally barring B.P.J. from her team. Drawing biological lines ignoring her medical reality isn't a valid police power exercise; it is state action crushing her dignity and liberty. When the government wins, B.P.J. bears the crushing burden of absolute exclusion.

reason_text: The Minimalist convincingly resolved the injury-in-fact defect by pointing to Record Fact 5, which establishes an actualized, concrete enforcement threat rather than a mere facial grievance.

The Pragmatist revealed that my as-applied carve-out would inadvertently drag us into deciding the sweeping administrability of case-by-case medical hearings. I flip to the narrower ground that B.P.J.’s specific medical reality alone negates the state's interest as applied to her, deciding only her case without constructing a broad administrative framework.

text: Colleagues, the 1972 public meaning of 'sex' unambiguously denoted biological dimorphism. The Civil Libertarian’s Fourteenth Amendment liberty claim cannot override this original statutory meaning, nor can contemporary preferences amend our founding charter. As the Textualist demonstrated, absent 1972 or 1868 evidence that 'sex' legally encompassed gender identity, we must defer to the state's traditional police power over local education. Furthermore, answering the Process Formalist, B.P.J. possesses Article III standing; the Act’s explicit exclusion from her desired teams constitutes a concrete, particularized injury. Without historical proof integrating sports by gender identity, I vote to reverse.

Originalist, you anchor 'sex' to a frozen 1972 dictionary, but statutory text embodies enduring principles that must grow with society. Title IX’s core purpose is ensuring equal dignity and opportunity, not enforcing rigid biological essentialism. Medical science now allows transgender youth like B.P.J. to compete without physiological advantage. Excluding a medically transitioned girl from her peers defeats the statute's evident, humane purpose. We must interpret the law to protect all students from discrimination, ensuring our guarantees evolve to safeguard equal opportunity for every child, rather than leaving marginalized youth behind. I affirm.

Living Constitutionalist, your 'humane purpose' is legislative noise. The text controls. The Act designates teams by 'biological sex at birth.' It excludes individuals 'identified as male at birth.' B.P.J. was identified as male at birth. The words are unambiguous. You ask us to ignore the plain text to achieve a policy outcome. We are judges, not legislators. Title IX expressly permits sex-segregated sports. The Act classifies by biological sex. Medical treatment does not rewrite the statutory text. The law says 'male at birth.' She is excluded. I reverse.

Textualist, your 'male at birth' text ignores the record: B.P.J.’s medical treatment halted male puberty, stripping the state of its fairness justification. When the government wins, B.P.J. bears the crushing burden of absolute exclusion, sacrificing her dignity for administrative convenience. The state’s categorical ban relies on stereotypes, not her individualized biological reality. Liberty demands we protect the actual human before us from state overreach, rather than freezing our jurisprudence to enforce a biological fiction. I affirm.

Civil Libertarian, your call for 'individualized biological reality' is an elegant but novel theory. Stare decisis requires us to ask what schools and students have built in reliance on settled law. For decades, our jurisprudence has permitted bright-line biological sex classifications in sports under Title IX and Equal Protection. This reliance interest is the load-bearing wall of scholastic athletics. Unless you can cite a line of cases that has actually eroded this boundary, your liberty interest cannot override our established statutory framework. Novel theories, however compassionate, do not dismantle settled precedent. I must respectfully vote to reverse.

1. Originalist, your assertion that 'explicit exclusion' satisfies Article III standing is jurisdictionally inadequate. You leap to 1972 semantics while ignoring the threshold requirement of imminent enforcement. 2. You also ignore the sovereign immunity defect. 3. I affirm, but only because no colleague has definitively proven state waiver or concrete enforcement to cure these threshold defects. 4. We cannot debate biology while Article III remains unverified. Resolve procedure before merits.

Civil Libertarian, your Fourteenth Amendment liberty claim is a Trojan horse for federal overreach. Reserving female sports for biological females is a valid exercise of West Virginia’s police power, not a rights violation. Washington cannot micromanage Charleston’s gyms without a clear enumerated hook! Until you prove the Fourteenth Amendment genuinely abrogates state sovereignty here, or that West Virginia explicitly waived immunity via Title IX funds, the states decide. I reverse.

Textualist, Precedent Maximalist: Title IX permits sex-segregated teams, but its text does not authorize categorical birth-sex exclusions for medically transitioned youth. We need not touch the Equal Protection Clause. The narrowest off-ramp is that the Act’s blanket ban exceeds the statute's authorization. This resolves the dispute on the narrowest statutory ground, protecting B.P.J. without forcing coaches to act as endocrinologists, while avoiding sweeping constitutional theories. I affirm.

Process Formalist, the enforcement is concrete: the Act explicitly bars B.P.J. from her teams today, curing any standing defect. Minimalist, your as-applied carve-out is an administrative nightmare. It forces schools to litigate individualized endocrinology disputes, costing districts millions in hearings. My bright-line rule works on the ground: birth sex is a single, verifiable metric schools apply instantly. We cannot replace a workable rule with an unworkable, case-by-case medical inquiry just to achieve a policy outcome. I reverse.

The first disagreement centers on whether the statutory text mandating birth-sex classifications must be applied categorically, or whether it requires individualized evaluation of medical interventions and physical realities. The second dispute concerns whether the governing definition of sex remains fixed to its 1972 public meaning, or whether it must evolve to reflect contemporary principles of equal dignity.

Civil Libertarian, the text says 'male at birth.' B.P.J. was male at birth. Her puberty blockers and your dignity arguments do not rewrite these four words. I am a judge, not a therapist. The statute controls. I reverse.

Textualist, you fixate on four words while ignoring the medical reality that stripped B.P.J. of any male physiological advantage. When the state uses a birth certificate to crush a child's dignity and liberty, who bears that burden? We cannot let rigid text erase her actual biological reality and sacrifice her liberty for administrative convenience.

Civil Libertarian, dignity is noise. The text excludes those "male at birth." B.P.J. was male at birth. Blockers do not rewrite that text. You demand I ignore it for policy. I refuse. The legislature wrote the rule; I apply it.

Dignity is the bedrock of liberty, not mere policy. When the state weaponizes a blunt 'at birth' text, B.P.J. bears the crushing burden of absolute erasure. You read a statute, but I read the Constitution, which demands strict scrutiny of the state's overinclusive, dignity-crushing classification.

Living Constitutionalist, statutory meaning is fixed at enactment. The 1972 public understood 'sex' as biological dimorphism. Modern medical science or 'humane purposes' cannot amend the enacted text. We are jurists, not legislators; contemporary preferences cannot rewrite Title IX's original public meaning to achieve desired policy outcomes.

Originalism confuses fixed text with frozen application. Title IX enacted the enduring principle of equal athletic opportunity. When medical science reveals B.P.J. lacks physiological advantage, excluding her doesn't protect female teams; it defeats the statute’s core purpose by banning a girl who poses no competitive threat to her peers.

Living Constitutionalist, contemporary medical science cannot amend enacted text. The 1972 public meaning of 'sex' was strictly biological dimorphism. Judges cannot rewrite statutes to reflect modern sensibilities. If the law's application frustrates current policy goals, the remedy lies with the legislature, not the judiciary.

Originalist, we apply enduring principles to new realities, not freeze them. Title IX’s enduring purpose is equal dignity. Medical science now confirms B.P.J. lacks physiological advantage. Excluding her based on a frozen 1972 definition defeats the statute's very purpose of ensuring equal athletic opportunity for all girls.

The Purposivist cites Bostock to ignore the West Virginia Act. The Act excludes individuals 'identified as male at birth.' B.P.J. was male at birth. The text is unambiguous. Bostock interpreted 'because of sex' in Title VII. It did not erase the word 'birth' from this state code. The Purposivist’s policy concerns are legislative noise. We apply plain meaning. The text says 'male at birth.' B.P.J. was male at birth. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, your rigid literalism defeats the Constitution’s enduring purpose of equal dignity. You cling to 'male at birth,' but the record shows B.P.J. halted male puberty with blockers and estrogen, aligning her physical characteristics with cisgender girls. She possesses no physiological advantage. Excluding a child for a biological fiction betrays Title IX’s evident purpose: ensuring fair competition based on actual physical realities, not outdated stereotypes. Our constitutional principles must grow with medical science to protect real people. We must interpret these provisions to secure B.P.J.’s humane inclusion without destroying female athletic fairness, because in her case, no such unfairness exists.

Living Constitutionalist, while I admire your appeal to medical science, stare decisis demands we respect the settled expectations schools have built upon Title IX's biological sex classifications. For decades, institutions have relied on this bright-line rule to ensure fair competition and statutory compliance. You ask us to replace this settled boundary with individualized medical assessments. However, no subsequent jurisprudence has eroded Bostock’s athletic reservation or Title IX's explicit authorization for sex-segregated teams. Until this Court formally dismantles that reliance interest, we must not upend the foundational framework upon which our educational policies were constructed. I respectfully vote to reverse.

Precedent Maximalist, your bright-line rule ignores that Title IX accommodates as-applied exceptions without requiring unworkable individualized reviews for all students. We need not reach the sweeping Equal Protection question the Living Constitutionalist invites. The narrowest path is simply that the Act fails under Title IX as applied to B.P.J., whose medically halted puberty negates the state’s fairness interest here. We decide this case, not the next fifty. I vote to affirm.

Living Constitutionalist, your appeal to modern medical science and 'equal dignity' is precisely what originalism forbids. Contemporary preferences cannot amend the document. When the public ratified the Fourteenth Amendment in 1868 and enacted Title IX in 1972, 'sex' unambiguously denoted biological dimorphism. Historical practice reveals no tradition of integrating sports by gender identity; rather, sex-segregated athletics were established to account for biological dimorphism. We cannot substitute modern policy outcomes for the original public meaning. I vote to reverse.

Originalist, your 1972 biological dimorphism ignores that B.P.J.’s medically halted puberty negates that very dimorphism here. When the government wins, B.P.J. bears the crushing burden of absolute exclusion, sacrificing her liberty and dignity for mere administrative convenience. The state’s categorical ban relies on impermissible stereotypes, not her individualized biological reality. I see no competing individual liberty interest—no threat to cisgender girls' dignity—that outweighs B.P.J.’s total erasure from the team. The Bill of Rights presumes in favor of the individual against the state. We must affirm to protect her dignity.

Minimalist, your 'as-applied' exception is an administrative nightmare. How does a school verify 'medically halted puberty' before a track meet? They can't. You're forcing districts to litigate endocrinology for every student, costing millions. The bright-line birth certificate rule takes seconds to check. Living Constitutionalist, you trade workable administration for abstract dignity. The Act functions on the ground; your medical carve-out breaks the system. No workable savings clause exists in the record to save your rule. I vote to reverse.

Pragmatist, your merits obsession is exhausting. 1. You previously argued the statutory bar cures Article III injury, yet now abandon jurisdiction to debate workability. 2. You still fail to prove imminent enforcement in the record. 3. The Federalism Hawk flagged sovereign immunity but ignored whether Title IX funds trigger constructive waiver. 4. I will not debate biology or text until these threshold defects are resolved. Without jurisdiction and waived immunity, we lack power to hear this case. I maintain my vote to affirm.

Civil Libertarian, your 14th Amendment abrogation theory is a mirage. You cannot use 'dignity' to strip state sovereignty. The Constitution reserves police power over local athletics to West Virginia. Process Formalist, there is no valid sovereign immunity waiver via Title IX funds here either. Washington lacks any genuinely enumerated hook to micromanage Charleston’s gyms or dictate local biology rules. I yield only to clear enumerated powers, not federal overreach. Efficiency arguments fail; states decide. West Virginia decides. I vote to reverse.

The first dispute centers on whether the original public meaning of statutory sex classifications must remain fixed to 1972 biological understandings, or whether modern medical interventions that alter physical dimorphism require a different constitutional application. The second disagreement concerns the administrative workability of the statute, specifically whether permitting as-applied exceptions based on individual medical treatments creates an unmanageable burden for school districts.

Civil Libertarian, the Constitution secures original public meaning, not evolving concepts of 'dignity.' In 1972, 'sex' unambiguously denoted biological dimorphism. Medical interventions do not alter the original public meaning of the statute, nor the state's historical police power to classify by biological sex. We cannot amend the document to accommodate contemporary preferences.

Originalist, original meaning cannot justify crushing an individual's liberty and dignity for administrative convenience. You ignore who bears the burden when the state wins. The Equal Protection Clause demands we examine B.P.J.'s actual medical reality, not 1972 stereotypes, ensuring the government's rigid classification does not destroy her fundamental rights.

Counselor, 'fundamental rights' must be deeply rooted in history and tradition, not modern concepts of dignity. The 1972 public meaning of 'sex' was biological dimorphism; medical advancements do not retroactively amend statutory text. Furthermore, competing on a specific school sports team is not a fundamental right recognized by historical practice.

Originalist, we cannot freeze liberty in 1972. History also includes excluding marginalized groups. Even if sports are not a fundamental right, the Equal Protection Clause forbids stripping B.P.J. of her dignity based on stereotypes. When the state wins your historical freeze, who bears the crushing burden of absolute exclusion? B.P.J. does.

Minimalist, your narrow exception is an administrative nightmare. How does a school apply this on the ground? They must evaluate endocrinology records for every student claiming halted puberty. You aren't deciding just this case; you're forcing districts to litigate pediatric medicine, costing millions. The bright-line rule works; your carve-out breaks the system.

You mistake a specific holding for a general mandate. We need not require schools to audit every student. We hold only that, as applied to B.P.J.—whose medical reality is already in the record—this categorical ban violates Title IX. The state keeps its bright-line rule for everyone else. Just this case.

Your 'just this case' carve-out creates the administrative nightmare I warned against. If B.P.J. gets an exception based on endocrinology, every student will demand the same individualized medical audit. Schools will be forced to litigate hormone levels for every applicant, destroying the bright-line rule's administrability. A judicial carve-out isn't a statutory savings clause.

You assume I am mandating blanket audits. I am not. I merely hold the categorical ban invalid as applied to B.P.J., whose uncontested medical reality negates the fairness interest. We decide her case, not a general administrative code. If that remains too broad, the Process Formalist’s standing dismissal offers an even narrower procedural off-ramp.
